subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Time Dilation Near Massive Objects
description: One-minute skill: Time Dilation Near Massive Objects.

hook.text: Time runs slower near massive objects. A clock on a neutron star ticks slower than one floating in deep space.

buildup.text: Einstein's general relativity predicts this. Gravity warps spacetime, and time is part of the fabric that gets stretched.

discovery.text: GPS satellites must correct for this. Clocks in orbit run faster than those on Earth's surface by 38 microseconds per day.

twist.text: Near a black hole, time nearly stops for outside observers. You'd age normally while the universe fast-forwards around you.

climax.text: Gravitational redshift—light climbing out of a gravity well loses energy and shifts red—is the visible proof of this effect.

punchline.text: Gravity slows time. The deeper the well, the slower the clock ticks.

quiz.question: How does time dilation affect GPS satellites?
options: Clocks in orbit run faster than on Earth's surface and must be corrected daily
GPS clocks stop working near the Earth's poles due to stronger gravity
Satellites experience no time dilation because they orbit above the atmosphere
